title: Śrī Bharahesar nī Sajjhāy (The Litany of Exemplary Souls)
summary: The Bharahesar Sajjhāy is a study-recitation (sajjhāy / svādhyāya) made up of a long roll-call of names: first of great monks and heroic souls (verses 1–7), then of great satīs — virtuous, steadfast women (verses 8–13). It is not a prayer of request but a remembrance: by speaking the names of those who lived the path nobly, the reciter draws their virtues to mind and is inspired to imitate them. The tradition holds that the very act of uttering these names dissolves accumulated wrong-doing and steadies the heart, which is why it closes the morning (Rāi) reflection.
ritualPlacement: This sajjhāy belongs to the Rātrika (Rāi) pratikramaṇa, the daily pre-dawn reflection on the faults of the night. In the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) book it falls in the closing study-portion of Rāi, after the Kevalnāṇī Bṛhat Caityavandana and before the closing maṅgalācaraṇa verses (Maṅgalaṁ Bhagavān Vīro…) and the colophon. Note two framing facts, stated once here: (a) a sajjhāy is a svādhyāya — sacred study-recitation — and is NOT one of the six Āvaśyakas; the Rāi observance reuses Devasi's core Āvaśyaka sūtras and simply adds this sajjhāy as its own material. (b) Unlike the shared Āvaśyaka sūtras (e.g. the atiyār), this sajjhāy is proper to Rāi alone; it is not carried into Pakkhī/Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī and is not subject to the pakkhī→cāumāsī→saṁvatsarī word-substitution.
authorAndSource: Composer not named in the source book and not securely attributed in the wider tradition; the gāthās are received as an old, anonymous Prakrit (Ardhamāgadhī/Māhārāṣṭrī-tinged) composition transmitted within the Śvetāmbara svādhyāya corpus. The named exemplars are drawn from the canonical and narrative literature (Āvaśyaka, Uttarādhyayana, and the kathā tradition). Treat any single-author attribution as uncertain — see notes.
structureArc: The sutra is a two-panel litany, each panel closed by a 'fruit' (phala) verse. Panel I — great souls (mahāsattvas), mostly monks and renunciants: vv.1–6 name them in rapid succession, opening with Bharata and Bāhubali and running through Abhayakumāra, Ḍhaṇḍhaṇa, Sthūlibhadra, Vajra-ṛṣi, Jambū, Gajasukumāla, Avantisukumāla, Megha-kumāra and many more; v.7 is the closing phala — 'these great souls, joined to hosts of virtues, may they grant well-being; at the taking of whose names the bonds of sin dissolve.' Panel II — great satīs (mahāsatīs): vv.8–11 name virtuous women, opening with Sulasā, Candanabālā, Manoramā, Madanarekhā, Damayantī, and continuing through Rājimatī, Mṛgāvatī, Draupadī, Kuntī and the chief queens of Kṛṣṇa (v.11); v.12 names the seven sisters of Sthūlibhadra (Yakṣā, Yakṣadinnā, Bhūtā, Bhūtadinnā, Seṇā, Veṇā, Reṇā); v.13 is the closing phala — 'these great satīs, adorned with spotless śīla, are ever victorious; whose drum of fame still sounds across the whole three worlds.'
recurringImagery: One device governs the whole sutra: nāma-grahaṇa — 'name-taking' as a spiritual act. Rather than describe deeds, the verses simply list names; remembrance itself is the practice, on the principle that recalling the virtuous binds the reciter to their virtue and loosens the 'bonds of sin' (pāva-pabandhā / pāpa-prabandha, v.7). The two halves are deliberately mirrored: a long catalogue of men of valour and renunciation (vv.1–6) sealed by a phala verse (v.7), answered by a long catalogue of steadfast women (vv.8–12) sealed by its own phala verse (v.13). The final image is the jasa-paḍaha — the 'drum of fame' (yaśas-paṭaha) that still resounds throughout the tihuaṇa (tribhuvana, the three worlds), asserting that the renown of spotless śīla outlasts the ages.
keyThemes: remembrance of exemplary souls as spiritual practice
great monks and heroes (mahāsattvas) of vows and renunciation
great satīs (mahāsatīs) of spotless śīla and steadfastness
parallel honouring of monastic and lay, men and women
inspiration through example rather than petition

glossary.definition: Sacred study-recitation; the closing study-portion of a pratikramaṇa. It is NOT one of the six Āvaśyakas.
The daily pre-dawn reflection and atonement for the faults of the night, within which this sajjhāy is recited.
A 'great being' — a soul of supreme courage and virtue; the men/monks honoured in vv.1–7.
A 'great satī' — a woman of spotless conduct, chastity, and spiritual steadfastness; the women honoured in vv.8–13.
'Name-taking'; the act of reciting the names of the virtuous as remembrance and inspiration — the organizing device of this sutra.
Right conduct / chastity; the defining virtue of the satīs, called akalaṅka ('stainless') in v.13.
Spotless, stainless, without blemish — qualifying the śīla of the great satīs.
The 'bonds' or accumulated continuity of sin which, v.7 declares, dissolve at the utterance of these names.
A 'host of virtues'; the exemplars are described as joined to (saṁyukta) such virtue-multitudes (v.7).
The 'drum of fame'; the metaphor of v.13 — the renown of spotless śīla still resounds throughout the worlds.
The 'three worlds' (upper, middle, lower) across which the satīs' fame is said to sound.
A closing 'fruit' verse stating the benefit of the recitation; here vv.7 and 13 seal the two panels of the litany.

howToRead: A newcomer can simply receive it as a hall of heroes and heroines: read each name knowing it stands for a remembered life of courage, renunciation, or unshakeable conduct, and let vv.7 and 13 tell you why the remembrance matters. An advanced practitioner can attend to the deliberate symmetry — the monastic/heroic panel answered by the satī panel, each sealed by its phala-gāthā — and use the names as a meditative roll-call linking the day's resolve to specific canonical exemplars, without re-glossing each name (the per-verse commentaries do that).

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Ritual placement of the Bharahesar sajjhāy within the Rāi pratikramaṇa and its status as svādhyāya, not an Āvaśyaka.

notes: Authorship is uncertain: the source book does not name a composer, and the wider tradition transmits these gāthās as anonymous. Web sources conflating this with a separate 'Bharahesar Bāhubali' stuti to Bāhubali/Gommaṭeśvara are unreliable and were not relied upon. The exemplar names span canonical and kathā literature; individual identifications (and minor name/spelling and verse-count variations, e.g. the exact roster of satīs in vv.8–12) differ across recensions — defer to the book's verbatim readings, including its orthographic peculiarities (e.g. atiyār spelled with ય). Where a specific name's story is unverified, the per-verse commentary should flag it rather than invent it.
